message:
fix(core): disable QF build-on-first-consult to eliminate bench regression (IMPL-9a refinement)

Root cause of 369x regression on update-deletethroughput__100-rows-delete-5-rows
(BENCH-REGRESSION-TRIAGE / task #125): qf_maybe_short_circuit_for_rowid's
build-on-first-consult path triggered a full-table rowid scan at first DELETE/UPDATE
after Connection::open. For a 100-row table, the scan cost ~30ms per op vs the
actual delete's ~0.1ms, dominating wall-clock.

The filter is only strictly beneficial when populated incrementally from INSERTs
in the same connection (AG-1C's original intent: absent-key DELETE rejection on
hot INSERT workloads). For cross-connection DML on existing tables, the consult
is strictly additive overhead because the caller descends anyway when the filter
answers "maybe".

Change: qf_maybe_short_circuit_for_rowid now returns Ok(false) when no filter
entry exists, rather than eagerly building one. qf_record_insert still populates
any already-built entry. quotient_filter_seed_from_cursor is retained (dead-code
allowed) as a primitive for a future explicit-build trigger (e.g., on first
INSERT of a freshly-created table, where build cost is O(0) rows).

Verified via rch worker: cargo check -p fsqlite-core clean.

Follow-up: design an activation policy that explicitly builds the filter when
build cost is known-zero (fresh table), then mark built. Track in a new
BENCH-BASELINE task after re-measurement confirms the regression is gone.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
